title: The Pillay Family Trail: Pewley Down Signallers
blurb: Climb Guildford's own grassy hilltop! Two hundred years ago, messages were flashed from a tower up here all the way to Navy ships in Portsmouth. Find the old signalling house, read a special thank-you stone, spot St Martha's church across the valley and hunt for butterflies on the chalk meadow. Big skies, big views — Isaac cracks the clues, Eli runs and rolls. ~2.5 km, flexes 45–90 min.
difficulty: family · ages 2 & 6 · open grassy hilltop · gentle climb · flexes 45–90 min

start.name: Top of Pewley Hill, by Semaphore House
start.directions: Walkable from town/home up Pewley Hill (no car park — limited, considerate street parking near the top of Pewley Hill / Semaphore Road only). ⚠️ OPEN & SUNNY — best done EARLY on a hot day: hats, suncream and water for everyone. A gentle grassy climb; for the buggy you'll want all-terrain wheels or a sling for Eli on the grass. SAFETY: Semaphore House (stop 1) is a PRIVATE home — admire it from the public road/path only. Great for kite-flying and rolling down the grass (if it's dry!).

stops.name: The Signalling House
stops.clue: At the top of the hill stands an old house with a funny little round tower (a 'cupola') on its roof. Two hundred years ago, BEFORE phones or radios, people here flashed messages using giant wooden arms — all the way from London to the Navy in Portsmouth! What is the house called: Semaphore House, or Sausage House?
stops.answer_hint: It's the same word as the arm-waving signals — sem-a-phore!
stops.little_ones: Eli — wave your arms up and down like a signal: big arms, send a message! 🦾
stops.grownup: An Admiralty semaphore station was built on Pewley Hill in 1822, part of the London–Portsmouth line that relayed naval messages hilltop-to-hilltop in minutes. It survives as 'Semaphore House' (the rooftop cupola was added after it closed).
stops.story: Before phones, messages were sent by 'semaphore' — big wooden arms on towers that spelled out letters. This station (1822) passed messages from London to Navy ships in Portsmouth, hill to hill!

stops.name: Top of the Down
stops.clue: Walk out onto the big open grassy hilltop — this is Pewley Down. It's so high you can see for miles! Find a good spot on the grass and check in, Signallers.
stops.little_ones: Eli — run on the big grassy hill and roll like a sausage (if it's dry)! 🌱
stops.grownup: Pewley Down is open chalk grassland on the edge of Guildford — rich in wildflowers and butterflies, with sweeping North Downs views.
stops.story: Pewley Down is a big open grassy hilltop — brilliant for running, kite-flying and looking out for miles in every direction.

stops.name: The Thank-You Stone
stops.clue: Find the stone marker that looks like a little stone pillar. This whole hilltop was GIVEN to the people of Guildford as a 'playground for ever' to remember a great war. What year was it given? (Clue: World War One ended in 1918 — this was two years later.)
stops.little_ones: Eli — find the special stone and give it a gentle pat, then say a big 'thank you' to the hill! 🪨
stops.grownup: On 29 July 1920 Pewley Down was given to Guildford to mark the end of the First World War, to be 'preserved for time immemorial as a playground for the people of Guildford'. The trig-point-like memorial stone was renewed by the town in the 1990s.
stops.story: After the First World War, this hill was given to the people of Guildford in 1920 — to stay wild and free as a playground for ever. The stone says thank you.

stops.name: Spot St Martha's!
stops.clue: Look SOUTH across the valley — can you spot a wooded hill with a tiny stone church right on top? That's St Martha's Hill! (Maybe you've climbed it.) Find the view and wave to the pilgrims over there.
stops.little_ones: Eli — point at the faraway hills and do your BIGGEST wave to St Martha's! 👋
stops.grownup: From Pewley Down the view south takes in St Martha's Hill and Chantry Wood — a lovely link to the St Martha's Pilgrims trail. On a clear day the North Downs roll away for miles.
stops.story: From up here you can see St Martha's Hill with its little church on top — the very one from the Pilgrims trail. Two hilltops waving across the valley!

stops.name: The Butterfly Meadow
stops.clue: Pewley Down is special CHALK grassland — in summer it fills with wildflowers and fluttering wings. Which would you really find here on a sunny day: butterflies, or sharks?
stops.answer_hint: Sharks need the sea! Look for the colourful fluttery ones instead.
stops.little_ones: Eli — flutter like a butterfly and find a yellow or purple flower! 🦋
stops.grownup: Chalk downland like Pewley Down is one of the richest habitats for wildflowers and butterflies in the UK — look for marbled whites and common blues on a warm day.
stops.story: Chalk hills like this fill with wildflowers and butterflies in summer. How many different coloured butterflies can you count before home time?

finale.title: Signallers Isaac & Eli — message received! 📡🎉
finale.message: You found the semaphore house, conquered the down, read the 1920 thank-you stone, spotted St Martha's across the valley and hunted butterflies. Champion hilltop signallers! Show Mum and Dad all your stickers.
finale.reward_idea: An ice cream back down in town — signalled and approved! 🍦

credits: Map data © OpenStreetMap contributors · facts © Wikipedia (CC BY-SA) / The Guildford Dragon
_recce_notes: PREP 2026-06-20, close-to-home option (walkable from town/home — NO car park; limited considerate street parking near the top of Pewley Hill / Semaphore Road, or walk up from town/the Wey). OPEN & SUNNY — best done EARLY on a hot day; hats/suncream/water. Gentle hill; grass paths so buggy = all-terrain or sling for Eli. SOFT pins to FOOT-VERIFY + retune geofences: Semaphore House (1, ~51.2378,-0.5631 — PRIVATE house, view from road/path only), Top of the Down (2), memorial stone (3, ~51.2348,-0.5548 — confirm exact spot + that the year is legible), viewpoint (4), meadow (5). Pewley Down Nominatim anchor 51.2309,-0.5572 (down centroid, a little S). Open hilltop = good GPS. Durable clues: Semaphore House name + 1920 memorial stone. SAFETY: stop 1 is a private residence — admire from the public road only.
